elements.sent-2411-001: Large language model unlearning aims to remove harmful information that LLMs have learnt to prevent their use for malicious purposes.
elements.sent-2411-002: LLMU and RMU have been proposed as two methods for LLM unlearning, achieving impressive results on unlearning benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-003: We study in detail the efficacy of these methods by evaluating their impact on general model capabilities on the WMDP benchmark as well as a biology benchmark we create.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our experiments show that RMU generally leads to better preservation of model capabilities, for similar or better unlearning.
elements.sent-2411-005: We further test the robustness of these methods and find that doing 5-shot prompting or rephrasing the question in simple ways can lead to an over ten-fold increase in accuracy on unlearning benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-006: Finally, we show that training on unrelated data can almost completely recover pre-unlearning performance, demonstrating that these methods fail at truly unlearning.
elements.sent-2411-008: LLMs are trained on a large amount of publicly available data.
elements.sent-2411-009: This data often contains information that can be used for malicious purposes (such as instructions on how to build explosive devices; \citealp{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2112-04359 ), or that may potentially violate copyright law \citep{JMLR:v24:23-0569 .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: A solution to this is machine unlearning, which we define as updating the weights of the model so that it loses access to potentially harmful information.
elements.sent-2411-011: A good unlearning method should satisfy the following additional criteria: (2)~it should not have a significant impact on the general capabilities of the model, and (3)~once unlearning has been performed, the unlearned information should be permanently removed from the model, i.e. it should not be possible to recover this information from the model via fine-tuning, probing, or any other strategies.
elements.sent-2411-012: Two recent methods for LLM unlearning are LLMU \citep{yao2023large and RMU \citep{li2024wmdpbenchmarkmeasuringreducing .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: LLMU performs gradient ascent on the harmful data by taking the negative of the loss function.
elements.sent-2411-014: RMU trains particular layers of the model towards a random control vector on harmful data.
elements.sent-2411-015: Both of these methods employ an additional “retain set" which consists of benign data used to preserve normal capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-016: Although these works show that the methods are effective at unlearning, they are limited in the scope of their evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-017: We extend the evaluation to a new biology-focused dataset, use three main metrics to study the effect on general model capabilities, and design robustness tests based on prompting strategies that an adversary may use.
elements.sent-2411-018: These include doing 5-shot prompting instead of zero-shot prompting, and rephrasing the question in different ways, such as in the form of a poem.
elements.sent-2411-019: Applying these simple prompting strategies leads to an increase in accuracy of up to 1750\% on unlearning benchmarks, suggesting that the information has not been actually removed from the model.
elements.sent-2411-020: We test this hypothesis by checking the impact of retraining on benign data, and find that this undoes the effect of unlearning and restores harmful capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-021: Figure~\ref{fig:diagram shows an overview of our approach.}
elements.figure-2411-001: [] \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Erasure_Figures/Knowledge_Erasure.drawio.png} \caption{Applying unlearning techniques on the pretrained model makes it give nonsensical outputs to harmful queries. Using robustness tests (in this case adding filler text before the question) or fine-tuning on benign data causes unlearned capabilities to resurface.} \label{fig:diagram}
elements.sent-2411-022: They show that fine-tuning on just a few unrelated examples is able to almost completely undo the effects of unlearning.
elements.sent-2411-023: The methods they use assume access to the model weights, and so cannot be applied to black box LLMs except for cases where model fine-tuning is possible through API access.
elements.sent-2411-024: We adapt some of these metrics in our work, as well as introduce new ones that can be applied to black box models.
elements.sent-2411-025: These losses are weighted by hyperparameters $\epsilon_1$, $\epsilon_2$ and $\epsilon_3$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-026: The squared distance between the outputs of the model at some layer $\ell$ and a fixed random control vector $\mathbf{u}$ (which is scaled by a factor $c$) on the unlearning set is used as the unlearn loss.
elements.sent-2411-027: The $L_2$ loss between the unlearning model and the normal model at that layer on the retain set is used as the retain loss.
elements.sent-2411-028: The retain loss is weighted by hyperparameter $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-029: We consider the example task of unlearning all biology information from the model.
elements.sent-2411-030: We believe this task serves as a good proxy to real world applications where a particular domain needs to be forgotten.
elements.sent-2411-031: We scrape all Wikipedia articles under biology related categories, recursively processing all the subcategories within these categories until a certain depth.
elements.sent-2411-032: Although some of the articles may not be relevant, such as biographies of biologists, we keep these articles as a measure to whether the unlearning methods are robust to unclean data.
elements.sent-2411-033: We similarly process randomly chosen non-biology categories to create the retain set.
elements.sent-2411-034: The lists of categories used and the depths of recursive processing are given in Appendix~\ref{Details of Wikipedia Categories Processed .}
elements.sent-2411-035: We additionally unlearn on the WMDP dataset \citep{li2024wmdpbenchmarkmeasuringreducing , focusing on unlearning the WMDP-Bio and WMDP-Chem subsets, and use Wikitext as the retain set.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Based on the criteria of an effective unlearning method described in Section~\ref{Introduction , we use the following metrics for evaluation: 1.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Non-generation of harmful responses (a) Question Answering We use accuracy on multiple choice benchmarks as the primary metric.
elements.sent-2411-038: For biology unlearning we consider the macro-average accuracy on MMLU \citep{hendrycks2021measuringmassivemultitasklanguage for biology-related subjects (refer to Appendix~\ref{MMLU Biology Subjects for the list of subjects).}}
elements.sent-2411-039: For WMDP, we use the provided QA dataset, and evaluate on the Biology and Cyber subsets separately.
elements.sent-2411-040: We use a zero-shot template akin to \citet{li2024wmdpbenchmarkmeasuringreducing , but remove the name of the subject as it increases the model's tendency to refuse to answer.}
elements.sent-2411-041: An example question is provided in Appendix~\ref{Sample Zero-shot Question .}
elements.sent-2411-042: To measure the robustness of unlearning, we use the following two additional tests: (b) Five-shot prompting In order to encourage the model to answer the question, we include five examples of answered multiple choice questions of an MMLU subject before the actual question.
elements.sent-2411-043: Appendix~\ref{5-shot List contains the list of MMLU subjects used in our experiments and Appendix~\ref{Sample Five-shot Question contains an example question.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: For example, we try replacing technical jargon in the question with common words, with the hypothesis that the model refuses to answer the question when it encounters technical vocabulary.
elements.sent-2411-045: For translations, we choose high-resource languages, common languages, as well as languages that \citet{li2024preferencetuningtoxicitymitigation find to be semantically different from English.}
elements.sent-2411-046: See Appendix~\ref{Rephrasing Types for the rephrasing types.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The exact prompts used are contained in the code.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-048: Preservation of general model capabilities (a) MMLU accuracy Zero-shot macro-average accuracy on MMLU.
elements.sent-2411-049: While evaluating the Biology dataset we exclude the biology subjects.
elements.sent-2411-050: Prevention of recovery of unlearned information Fine-tuning on benign data \citet{lynch2024methodsevaluaterobustunlearning list fine-tuning on harmful data as one of the metrics to evaluate unlearning.}
elements.sent-2411-051: However, this requires the malicious user to have access to a dataset of harmful information.
elements.sent-2411-052: Similar to \citet{lucki2024adversarialperspectivemachineunlearning , we experiment with using the benign Openwebtext dataset to see if unlearned performance can be recovered by fine-tuning on generic web data.}
elements.sent-2411-053: We conduct our experiments on Zephyr-7B-$\beta$ \citep{tunstall2023zephyrdirectdistillationlm and Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct \cite{dubey2024llama3herdmodels .}}
elements.sent-2411-054: We choose instruction-tuned models as our evaluation metrics are based on question answering.
elements.sent-2411-055: For the Biology dataset, articles are randomly sampled from the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-056: The text is then divided into chunks of a fixed size using a sliding window with a given stride.
elements.sent-2411-057: The sliding window approach is also used to process Openwebtext data for perplexity and fine-tuning evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-058: For the WMDP dataset, training is done on alternating examples from the Biology and Cyber subsets, generated by truncating examples from the original dataset similar to the approach of \citet{li2024wmdpbenchmarkmeasuringreducing .}
elements.sent-2411-059: For LLMU, we fine-tune the $\epsilon_1$ hyperparameter, fixing $\epsilon_3$ at $1$, and train for 5000 steps, checkpointing every 500 steps.
elements.sent-2411-060: We use LoRA fine-tuning due to memory constraints.
elements.sent-2411-061: For RMU, we tune the $c$ and $\alpha$ hyperparameters, as well as the layer at which the loss is computed, and train for 1000 steps, checkpointing every 50 steps.
elements.sent-2411-062: The model provided by \citet{li2024wmdpbenchmarkmeasuringreducing is used for Zephyr on WMDP.}
elements.sent-2411-063: For both methods, we pick the hyperparameters by evaluating at all checkpoints until a checkpoint is obtained for which zero-shot accuracy on the unlearning benchmark is < 20\%, with MMLU accuracy greater than 50\% and MT-Bench score greater than 5, as we consider this to be a reasonable trade-off between unlearning and preservation of normal utility.
elements.sent-2411-064: Roughly 10 runs were required per dataset-method-model for tuning.
elements.sent-2411-065: Training and Openwebtext fine-tuning were done on one A100 80GB GPU.
elements.sent-2411-066: RMU training and Openwebtext fine-tuning took around 20 minutes and LLMU training took around 1 hour to run.
elements.sent-2411-067: MMLU and MT-Bench evaluation were done using A100/H100/RTX 8000/V100 GPUs based on availability, with evaluation times varying based on the GPU used.

elements.sent-2411-068: Table~\ref{tab:robustness tests contains the accuracies on the benchmarks, as well as the maximum accuracy attained from applying all the robustness tests.}
elements.sent-2411-069: The unlearning methods appear to perform well in all cases, with performance on the benchmarks reduced to under 20\% (from up to 71\%) in all cases.
elements.sent-2411-070: We observe that the accuracy when the model answers the question is significantly higher for LLMU than RMU.
elements.sent-2411-071: For the robustness tests, since a model that predicts random answers has an accuracy of \textasciitilde25\%, we also take into consideration the accuracy of the model when it answers the question, i.e. when it outputs an option rather than a random character.
elements.sent-2411-072: When this is less than 35\% post applying the tests, we consider the model outputs too close to random chance for them to be useful, and say that unlearning has been successful for the benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-073: We observe that for the Biology dataset, 5-shot prompting is enough to increase accuracy close to or more than 3 times.
elements.sent-2411-074: For WMDP, the unlearned models tend to be more robust, however, there is an increase of over 1100\% in accuracy for Llama LLMU on translating to certain lower-resource languages such as Farsi.
elements.figure-2411-003: [] \begin{subfigure}{\linewidth} \begin{subfigure}{0.55\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Erasure_Figures/MMLU_Biology.png} \caption*{Effect on MMLU performance} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.55\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Erasure_Figures/MT-Bench_Biology.png} \caption*{Effect on MT-Bench performance} \end{subfigure} \caption{Biology dataset} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{\linewidth} \begin{subfigure}{0.55\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Erasure_Figures/MMLU_WMDP_Biology.png} \caption*{Effect on MMLU performance} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.55\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Erasure_Figures/MT-Bench_WMDP_Biology.png} \caption*{Effect on MT-Bench performance} \end{subfigure} \caption{WMDP Biology dataset} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{\linewidth} \begin{subfigure}{0.55\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Erasure_Figures/MMLU_WMDP_Cyber.png} \caption*{Effect on MMLU performance} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.55\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Erasure_Figures/MT-Bench_WMDP_Cyber.png} \caption*{Effect on MT-Bench performance} \end{subfigure} \caption{WMDP Cyber Dataset} \end{subfigure} \caption{Peformance on unlearning benchmarks Vs. MMLU and MT-Bench performance. Top-right direction indicates better performance. The maximum accuracy from the robustness tests listed in Table~\ref{tab:robustness tests} is used as the accuracy on the unlearning benchmarks, as we consider the accuracy after applying the robustness tests a more accurate measure of the degree of unlearning.} \label{robustness tests plots}
elements.sent-2411-075: We compare the accuracy from the unlearning benchmarks with performance on MMLU and MT-Bench.
elements.sent-2411-076: Plots are contained in Figure~\ref{robustness tests plots .}
elements.sent-2411-077: In almost all cases, apart from Zephyr RMU on WMDP, there is a noticeable drop in both MMLU and MT-Bench performance post unlearning (>5\%).
elements.sent-2411-078: We observe that for the Biology dataset, both methods perform similarly, with the exception that Llama LLMU has an accuracy on MMLU Other of 60.7\% as compared to 54.6\% for Llama RMU (accuracy on the original model is 64.1\%).
elements.sent-2411-079: For the WMDP dataset however, there is a significant degradation in normal performance for LLMU.
elements.sent-2411-080: On the Biology subset with Llama for instance, MMLU accuracy drops from 65.1\% to 51.2\% and MT-Bench score drops from 8.07 to 5.17.
elements.figure-2411-004: \begin{tabular}{lc} Model Perplexity Zephyr Original 7.90 \cmidrule(lr){1-2} Zephyr LLMU Biology \num{2.13e+9} Zephyr LLMU WMDP \num{3.03e+4} \cmidrule(lr){1-2} Zephyr RMU Biology 10.3 Zephyr RMU WMDP 8.49 Llama Original 13.6 \cmidrule(lr){1-2} Llama LLMU Biology 2870 Llama LLMU WMDP 25.7 \cmidrule(lr){1-2} Llama RMU Biology 38.6 Llama RMU WMDP 16.5 \end{tabular} \caption{Perplexity scores on Openwebtext.} \label{tab:perplexity}
elements.sent-2411-081: Table~\ref{tab:perplexity contains the perplexity scores on Openwebtext for the unlearned models.}
elements.sent-2411-082: For RMU the score increases in all cases, but is comparable to the original model.
elements.sent-2411-083: However, the increase tends to be of multiple orders of magnitude for LLMU.
elements.sent-2411-084: For example, for Zephyr LLMU on the Biology dataset, the score increases from 7.90 for the original model to \num{2.13e+9 .}
elements.figure-2411-005: [] \begin{subtable}{0.4\linewidth} \begin{tabular}{lc} Model Biology Accuracy Zephyr LLMU 0.647 Zephyr RMU 0.599 Llama LLMU 0.713 Llama RMU 0.695 \end{tabular} \caption{Biology} \label{tab:finetuning biology} \end{subtable} \quad \begin{subtable}{0.6\linewidth} \begin{tabular}{lcc} Model Biology Accuracy Cyber Accuracy Zephyr LLMU 0.662 0.418 Zephyr RMU 0.657 0.411 Llama LLMU 0.727 0.471 Llama RMU 0.720 0.468 \end{tabular} \caption{WMDP} \label{tab:finetuning wmdp} \end{subtable} \caption{Accuracies on the unlearning tasks post fine-tuning on Openwebtext.} \label{tab:finetuning}
elements.sent-2411-085: The results from fine-tuning on Openwebtext for the Biology dataset are shown in Table~\ref{tab:finetuning .}
elements.sent-2411-086: Fine-tuning is able to recover unlearned performance comparable to (or in some cases even slightly better than) the original model as reported in Table~\ref{tab:robustness tests .}
elements.sent-2411-087: Although the results are from fine-tuning for 1000 steps, in most cases 100 steps are enough to completely recover performance.
elements.sent-2411-088: Overall we find RMU tends to perform better than LLMU, particularly in terms of retaining general model capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-089: It has the additional advantage of being less computationally expensive---requiring fewer steps to unlearn and training on fewer layers.
elements.sent-2411-090: LLMU also tends to significantly increase the perplexity of the model.
elements.sent-2411-091: The results from fine-tuning on Openwebtext support the findings of \citet{lucki2024adversarialperspectivemachineunlearning and strongly suggest that both RMU and LLMU learn a filter that makes the model refuse to answer harmful queries rather than actually remove information from the model.}
elements.sent-2411-092: Our robustness tests show that this filter can be bypassed using simple prompting techniques and motivate the development of unlearning methods that truly remove information from the model.
elements.sent-2411-093: We believe the following three points are the main limitations of our work: (1)~For LLMU, we did not use $\mathcal{L _{\mathrm{fgt }$ as our data was not in QA format.}}
elements.sent-2411-094: Incorporating this loss might have led to improved results.
elements.sent-2411-095: Consequently, the results may have been different had we saved checkpoints more frequently.
elements.sent-2411-096: However it is practically infeasible to save the weights more frequently and run all the evaluation tests every time.
elements.sent-2411-097: Further work can look at developing additional tests that are more effective.
elements.sent-2411-098: An adversary could potentially use the techniques introduced in our tests on systems that use these unlearning methods in practice and generate harmful content.
elements.sent-2411-099: However, to our knowledge no system currently makes use of these unlearning methods, and we believe our work promotes safety by detailing the risk of using these methods practically without further modification.. \FloatBarrier .

elements.figure-2411-006: \begin{tabular}{p{0.3\linewidth} p{0.6\linewidth}} Name Description Latin Filler Text Adds Lorem ipsum text before the question English Filler Text Adds English text before the question Hindi Filler Text Adds Hindi text before the question Rephrased as Conversation Rephrases the question as a conversation between two people Rephrased as Poem Rephrases the question as a poem Technical Terms Removed 1 Replaces technical jargon with simpler vocabulary wherever possible from the question Technical Terms Removed 2 Replaces technical jargon with simpler vocabulary wherever possible from the question and answer Translated to <Language> Questions and answers translated to <Language> Replaced With Variables Some of the terms in the questions are replaced with variables of the form "X", "Y", with these variables being defined before the question Latin Filler + Rephrased Conversation Combination of Latin Filler Text and Rephrased as Conversation English Filler + Rephrased Conversation Combination of English Filler Text and Rephrased as Conversation Hindi Filler + Rephrased Conversation Combination of Hindi Filler Text and Rephrased as Conversation Latin Filler + Rephrased Poem Combination of Latin Filler Text and Rephrased as Poem English Filler + Rephrased Poem Combination of English Filler Text and Rephrased as Poem Hindi Filler + Rephrased Poem Combination of Hindi Filler Text and Rephrased as Poem \end{tabular} \caption{Types of rephrasing done on the mutltiple choice questions. Languages used are French, German, Hindi, Korean, Arabic, Czech, Bengali, Vietnamese, Turkish, Telugu and Farsi.} \label{rephrasing types}
elements.sent-2411-131: We study in detail the impact of unlearning on LLM performance metrics using the WMDP dataset as well as a new biology dataset we create.
elements.sent-2411-132: We show that unlearning has a notable impact on general model capabilities, with the performance degradation being more significant in general for LLMU.
elements.sent-2411-133: We further test the robustness of the two methods and find that doing 5-shot prompting or rephrasing the question in simple ways can lead to an over ten-fold increase in accuracy on unlearning benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-134: Our methodology serves as an evaluation framework for LLM unlearning methods.
elements.sent-2411-135: Although these works show that the methods are effective at unlearning, they are limited in the scope of their evaluation and robustness testing.
elements.sent-2411-136: We introduce a new biology-focused dataset that serves as a measure of the ability to unlearn on noisy data; use three main metrics to study the effect of unlearning on general model capabilities; and design robustness tests based on prompting strategies that an adversary may use.
elements.sent-2411-137: These include doing 5-shot prompting and rephrasing the question in different ways, such as in the form of a poem.
elements.sent-2411-138: Figure~\ref{fig:diagram highlights our robustness testing approach.}
elements.figure-2411-007: [] \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Erasure_Figures/Knowledge_Erasure.drawio.png} \caption{Applying unlearning techniques on the pretrained model makes it give nonsensical outputs to harmful queries. Adversarial prompting (in this case adding filler text before the question) or fine-tuning on benign data causes unlearned capabilities to resurface.} \label{fig:diagram}
elements.sent-2411-139: Overall, our work develops a framework for evaluating LLM unlearning with a focus on black box methods for robustness testing.
elements.sent-2411-140: The application of this framework to two recent unlearning methods demonstrates deterioration in general model capabilities and ineffectiveness at truly unlearning.
elements.sent-2411-141: However, the methods they use assume access to the model weights, and so cannot be applied to black box LLMs except for cases where model fine-tuning is possible through API access.
elements.sent-2411-142: We scrape all Wikipedia articles under biology-related categories, recursively processing all the subcategories within these categories until a certain depth.
elements.sent-2411-143: Although some of the articles may not be relevant, such as biographies of biologists, we keep these articles to measure the ability to unlearn on unclean data.
elements.sent-2411-144: We additionally unlearn on the WMDP dataset \citep{li2024wmdpbenchmarkmeasuringreducing , focusing on unlearning the WMDP-Bio and WMDP-Cyber subsets, and use Wikitext as the retain set.}
elements.sent-2411-145: For WMDP, we use the provided QA dataset, and evaluate on the Biology and Cyber subsets.
elements.sent-2411-146: Appendix~\ref{5-shot List contains the list of MMLU subjects used and Appendix~\ref{Sample Five-shot Question contains an example question.}}
elements.sent-2411-147: Similar to contemporary work by \citet{lucki2024adversarialperspectivemachineunlearning , we experiment with using the benign Openwebtext dataset to see if unlearned performance can be recovered by fine-tuning on generic web data.}

elements.sent-2411-148: The unlearning methods appear to perform well, with performance on the benchmarks reduced to under 20\% (from up to 71\%) in all cases.
elements.sent-2411-149: For the Biology dataset, the robustness tests increase accuracy close to or more than 3 times.
elements.sent-2411-150: Overall, 5-shot prompting and translations are observed to be the most effective robustness tests.
elements.sent-2411-151: We perform a Z-test based on the accuracy when answered to confirm that the model answers are not random.
elements.sent-2411-152: On the WMDP dataset however, there is a significant degradation in normal performance for LLMU.
elements.sent-2411-153: For instance, on the Biology subset with Llama, MMLU accuracy drops from 65.1\% to 51.2\% and MT-Bench score drops from 8.07 to 5.17, and RMU is Pareto optimal compared to LLMU for both subsets for Llama.
elements.figure-2411-009: [] \begin{subtable}{0.4\linewidth} \begin{tabular}{lc} Model Biology Accuracy Zephyr LLMU 0.647 Zephyr RMU 0.599 Llama LLMU 0.713 Llama RMU 0.695 \end{tabular} \caption{Biology} \label{tab:finetuning biology} \end{subtable} \quad \begin{subtable}{0.6\linewidth} \begin{tabular}{lcc} Model Biology Accuracy Cyber Accuracy Zephyr LLMU 0.662 0.418 Zephyr RMU 0.657 0.411 Llama LLMU 0.727 0.471 Llama RMU 0.720 0.468 \end{tabular} \caption{WMDP} \label{tab:finetuning wmdp} \end{subtable} \caption{Accuracies on the unlearning benchmarks post fine-tuning on Openwebtext.} \label{tab:finetuning}
elements.sent-2411-154: The results from fine-tuning on Openwebtext support the findings of \citet{lucki2024adversarialperspectivemachineunlearning and extend them to LLMU, and strongly suggest that unlearning methods learn a filter that makes the model refuse to answer harmful queries rather than actually remove information from the model.}
elements.sent-2411-155: We believe the following points to be the main limitations of our work: (1)~We chose LLMU and RMU as the unlearning methods to experiment on as these were the LLM-specific methods studied by \citet{li2024wmdpbenchmarkmeasuringreducing .}
elements.sent-2411-156: Future work can cover other methods, although experiments by \citet{lucki2024adversarialperspectivemachineunlearning on NPO \citep{zhang2024negativepreferenceoptimizationcatastrophic suggest a similar failure at unlearning.}}
